comments: Roger Waters drops in to play and discuss “The Wall” LP in it’s entirety.
A full transcript of this show can be read here

transcript.text: On 275 and 285. Also in stereo on VHF. This is National Radio 1.
shelter from pigs on the wing well hello there thomas the vance here tv on radio welcome to this
rather special edition of the friday rock show may i say at the beginning of this program many
thanks to everybody who voted for this program and this year have made it the top radio show
in the melody maker poll tony wilson and myself thank you very much well it was animals that came
out two and a half years ago and that was the last time that the pink floyd went on record so to
week tonight you can hear roger waters of the pink floyd going on record all about the double album
which has just got into the shops the double album called the wall we're going to play the
whole album and we're going to be led through it and around it and it's going to be explained by
the man who really did put it together did most of the work on it and wrote most of the songs
so now let me just hand the wall over to roger waters and the first question i asked him in
Hollywood in a hotel bedroom was how did the war come about well the idea for the war came from
10 years of touring with rock shows I think particularly the last few years when in 75
and in 77 we were playing to very large audiences some of whom were our old audience who can't who'd
come to hear what we wanted to play, but most of whom were only there for the beer, in big
stadiums, and consequently it became rather an alienating experience doing the shows.
And I became very conscious of a wall between us and our audience, and this record started
out as being an expression of those feelings.
But it goes, I think, a little bit deeper than that, because the record actually seems
the start at the beginning of the character's life the story has been developed considerably
since then this was two years ago i started to write it and now it's partly about a show
situation a live show situation in fact the album starts off in a live show and then it um
flashes back and traces a story of a character if you like of pink himself whoever he may be
but initially it was just stemmed from shows being horrible when you say horrible do you mean
that really you didn't want to be there yeah it's all um partially because the people who you're
most aware of at a rock show when you're on the stage are the front 20 or 30 rows of bodies
and uh in large situations where you're using what's euphemistically called festival seating
they tend to be packed together swaying madly and uh it's very difficult to perform in those
situations of people whistling and shouting and screaming and throwing things and hitting each
other and crashing about and letting off fireworks and you know i mean having a wonderful time but
it's a drag to try and play when that all that's going on but uh i felt at the same time that
it's a situation that we'd created ourselves through our own greed you know if you play
very large but the only real reason for playing very large venues is to make money but surely
in your case it wouldn't be economic or feasible to play a small venue well it would it's not going
be on when we do this show because with this show is going to lose money but on those tours that i'm
talking about the 75 tour of europe and england and and the 77 tour of europe england and america
as well we were making money we made a lot of money on those tours because we were playing
big venues what would you like the audience to do how would you like the audience to react to me
to your music i'm actually happy that they do whatever they find feel is necessary because
they're only expressing you know their response to what it's like in a way i'm saying that they're
right you know that those shows are bad news there is an idea or there has been an idea for
many years abroad that it's a very uplifting and wonderful experience and that there's a great
contact between the audience and the performers on the stage and i think that that is not
true i think i think that in very many cases um it's actually a rather alienating experience
for the audience for everybody it's two and a half years since you had a an album out i think
people will be interested in knowing how long it's taken you to develop this double album right well
we toured we did a tour which ended i think in july or august 77 and when we finished that tour
in in the autumn of that year that's when i started writing it it took took me a year
no until the next july working on my own and then and then i had a demo for sort of 90 minutes of
stuff which i played to the rest of the night and then we all started working on it together
in the October or November of that.
October 78, we started working on it together.
And you actually ceased recording, I think, in November of this year.
Yeah. We didn't start recording in November.
We didn't start recording until the new year, well, until April this year,
but we were rehearsing and fiddling about with it
and obviously rewriting a lot.
So it's taken a long time, but we always tend to work very slowly anyway
because it's difficult.
the first track is in the flesh yeah this actually sets up what the character has become
yes at the end couldn't have put it better myself it's a reference back to our 1977 tour which was
called pink floyd in the flesh and then you have a track called the thin ice yeah now this is i think
at the very very beginning of the character call the character pink around the very beginning of
pink's life yeah absolutely yeah he did in fact at the end of uh in the flesh um you you hear
somebody shouting roll the sound effects and uh you hear the sound of bombers so it gives you some
indication of what's happening in the show it'll be much more obvious what's going on so it's a
flashback and we start telling a story which uh in terms of this it's about my generation
the war yeah war babies but it could be about anybody who gets left by anybody if you like
did that happen to you yeah my father was killed in the war another brick in the wall part one
which is actually about the father who's gone yeah though the father in the has flown across
the ocean yeah now the assumption from listening to that would be that he's gone away to somewhere
else yeah well it could be you see it works on various levels it doesn't have to be about the
the war i mean i think it could you know it can it should work for any generation nearly the father
is also i'm the father as well you know people who who leave their families to go and work not

be a simple story about you know somebody getting killed in the war and going up and going to school
than an hour but about being left more generally the happiest days of our lives is uh a complete
condemnation as i see it as i've heard it in the album of somebody's scholastic career
well my my school life was very like that it was awful it was really terrible when i hear people
whining on now about bringing back grammar school it really makes me quite ill to listen to it
because I went to a boys' grammar school,
and although, I want to make it plain,
that some of the men who taught in, it was a boys' school,
some of the men who taught there were very nice guys.
You know, I'm not, it's not meant to be a blanket condemnation
of all teachers everywhere,
but the bad ones can really do people in.
And there were some at my school who were just incredibly bad
and treated the children so badly.
Just putting them down, just putting them down,
putting them down putting them down you know all the time never encouraging them to do things
not really trying to interest them in anything just trying to keep them quiet and still and
crush them into the right shape so that they would go on to university and do well
what sort of a mother is this mother overprotective which most mothers are if you can level one
accusation at mothers it is that they tend to protect their children too much too much and for
too long that's all this isn't a portrait of my mother although some of the you know one or two

enough lots of people recognize that and in fact um a woman that i know the other day who'd heard
the album called me up and said she liked it and she said that that she'd listening to that track
made her feel very guilty she's got sort of three kids and i wouldn't have said that she was
particularly overprotective towards her children but it's i was interested that it you know she's
woman of well my age and i was interested that it got through so i was glad it had you know i mean
if you can if it means that much to people i think it's good mother do you think they'll drop the bomb
mother do you think they'll like this song mother do you think they'll try to break my balls

What is actually happening at this stage in Pink's life?

This is the start of side two.

So, yes, it's remembering one's childhood

and then comes the track what shall we do now is this uh the assumption would be this is when

adopting somebody else's criteria for yourself

On this level, the story is extremely simplistic.
I hope that on other levels there are less tangible,
more effective things that come through.
I think it's OK in a show where you only hear the words.
We probably won't hear the words at all.
the way rock and roll shows get produced but they're there on the sleeve if you need them yeah
that's why we didn't go into a great panic about trying to change all the inner bags and things i

as the writer i mean was there ever a young lust section of your life well yes i suppose actually

because of being too frightened really now it's completely different that was a function of us
